content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
PO BOX 607 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Nov 12, 1977

Ms. Marilyn Mayer
c/o Office of the Attorney General
3580 Wilshire Boulevard
Los Angeles, California 90010

Dear Ms. Mayer:

This letter is in further reference to our telephone conversation
of May 10. Enclosed please find opinion request #77-010 which involves
the use of a blind trust to avoid the necessity of disqualification of
a public official under the Political Reform Act of 1974 and a conflict
of interest code promulgated pursuant to that Act. As we discussed,
there are several possible conclusions the Commission might arrive at
in analyzing the subject, to wit: that blind trusts can never negate
the provisions of Government Code Section 87100; that they do in all
circumstances; or that they is a middle ground with certain guidelines
being enunciated. At any rate, the question is an open one and the
staff would certainly appreciate your views or comments on the matter.
I will keep you informed of the progress of the opinion request. You
might also wish to consider submitting an opinion request yourself to the
extent you anticipate the actual matter materially differing from the facts
presented in the attached materials.

We also discussed the Moody opinion about pension plans. I have
reviewed the opinion and in the context relevant to our discussion
we discussed. It held that receipt of retirement benefits from a county
retirement system did not prevent a retired member of the system from
acting as a member of an agency dealing with administration of the system.
The payments were found to be an expansion of the employee's "salary"
and hence defined not to be "income" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act. See, Government Code Section 82016(1)(2).

The opinion, nor any advice previously issued by the Commission, did
not discuss the effect of contributions to a pension fund as opposed to
receipt of payments from such a fund. Government Code Section 82030
does, however, define as "income", "contribution to an insurance or pension
program paid by any person other than an employer (emphasis supplied).
The implication clearly is that if the employer pays such contributions
they are not income to the employee. Thus, the question of who is the
stewards employer may be crucial. If you request an opinion or advice on
that question, you should be prepared to submit to the Commission as much
information as possible to consider that problem.

p. 31

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION 916 ·· 95

Ms. Marilyn Mayer
May 12, 1977
page two

In light of the unresolved state of these issues, you may desire to
request an opinion and/or advice of the Commission pursuant to Government
Code Section 83114. If I can be of assistance in that or any matter, please
call me at (916) 322-6444.

Sincerely,

Kenneth W. Godwin
Research Specialist III
Conflicts of Interest Division

KWG:mfa
enclosure

sections.question_synthetic: The document discusses issues related to blind trusts and whether they can negate disqualification requirements under the Political Reform Act, and whether employer-paid pension contributions constitute 'income' under Government Code Section 82030.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC staff indicates the blind trust question remains open with multiple possible outcomes, notes that prior Moore opinion regarding pension benefits is distinguishable because it addressed receipt of retirement benefits (not contributions), and clarifies that Government Code Section 82030 defines employer-paid pension contributions as not income, leaving the determination of employer status as crucial.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is correspondence rather than formal advice letter response; no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present; letter references an enclosed opinion request and discusses potential legal positions without issuing final binding advice; tone is consultative rather than advisory

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The document discusses issues related to blind trusts and whether they can negate disqualification requirements under the Political Reform Act, and whether employer-paid pension contributions constitute 'income' under Government Code Section 82030.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC staff indicates the blind trust question remains open with multiple possible outcomes, notes that prior Moore opinion regarding pension benefits is distinguishable because it addressed receipt of retirement benefits (not contributions), and clarifies that Government Code Section 82030 defines employer-paid pension contributions as not income, leaving the determination of employer status as crucial.
embedding.first_500_words: May 12, 1977 Ms. Marilyn Mayer c/o Office of the Attorney General 3580 Wilshire Boulevard Los Angeles, California 90010 Dear Ms. Mayer: This letter is in further reference to our telephone conversation of May 10. Enclosed please find opinion request #77-010 which involves the use of a blind trust to avoid the necessity of disqualification of a public official under the Political Reform Act of 1974 and a conflict of interest code promulgated pursuant to that Act. As we discussed, there are several possible conclusions the Commission might arrive at in analyzing the subject, to wit: that blind trusts can never negate the provisions of Government Code Section 87100; that they do in all circumstances; or that there is a middle ground with certain guidelines being enunciated. At any rate, the question is an open one and the staff would certainly appreciate your views or comments on the matter. I will keep you informed of the progress of the opinion request. You might also wish to consider submitting an opinion request yourself to the extent you anticipate the CHIRB matter materially differing from the facts presented in the attached materials. We also discussed the Moore opinion about pension plans. I have reviewed that opinion and it does not appear relevant to the circumstances we discussed. It held that receipt of retirement benefits from a county retirement system did not prevent a retired member of the system from acting as a member of an agency dealing with administration of the system. The payments were found to be an extension of the employee's "salary" and hence defined not to be "income" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. See, Government Code Section 82030(b)(2). The opinion, nor any advice previously issued by the Commission, did not discuss the effect of contributions to a pension fund as opposed to receipt of payments from such a fund. Government Code Section 82030 does, however, define as "income", "contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any person other than an employer (emphasis supplied). The implication clearly is that if the employer pays such contributions they are not income to the employee. Thus, the question of who is the stewards employer may be crucial. If you request an opinion or advice on that question, you should be prepared to submit to the Commission as much information as possible concerning that problem. Ms. Marilyn Mayer May 12, 1977 page two In light of the unresolved state of these issues, you may desire to request an opinion and/or advice of the Commission pursuant to Government Code Section 83114. If I can be of assistant in that or any matter, please call me at (916) 322-6444. Sincerely, Kenneth W. Goshorn Research Specialist III Conflicts of Interest Division KWG:mfa enclosure
embedding.summary: This is a follow-up letter from FPPC Research Specialist Kenneth Goshorn to Ms. Marilyn Mayer regarding ongoing analysis of opinion request #77-010 concerning blind trusts and conflict of interest disqualification under the Political Reform Act, along with discussion of pension contributions and income definitions.
